data.title: HeartFlow: Growth Mirage In A Crowded Market
data.description:
HeartFlow, Inc. (NASDAQ:HTFL) presents an unattractive risk/reward proposition: its large and attractive market opportunity invites heightened competition; the business remains reliant on a single product for virtually all its revenue; it is open to pricing pressures from its key customers; it has a history of fast growth without sustainable value creation; and the share price is justifiable only if margins surge and the company grows at a high rate for an improbably long time. I assign a “Sell” rating to the company. .

Betting Everything on HeartFlow FFRCT Analysis

HeartFlow FFRCT Analysis, the firm’s product for calculating blood flow and determining clinically significant coronary artery disease (CAD), accounts for 99% of the firm’s revenue, with the firm working toward commercialising its second product, HeartFlow Plaque Analysis, which assesses coronary plaque in order to optimise treatment strategies. The firm has vowed to increase its sales of HeartFlow FFRCT Analysis while also raising sales of HeartFlow Plaque Analysis, in order to better diversify its revenue streams.

Customer Relationships Pose Risks Despite Broad Base

Although none of the company’s 1,100 customers contributes 10% or more of revenue, HeartFlow’s reliance on large health systems that operate many accounts creates exposure. The firm’s two largest customers collectively contributed 8% of revenue as of the end of 2024. Any deterioration in these relationships could adversely affect revenue, and this reality reduces the firm’s bargaining power. As management acknowledges:

These customers could also potentially pressure us to reduce the prices we charge for the HeartFlow Platform, which could have a material adverse effect on our margins and business.

This has, I think, implications for the firm’s ability to improve its NOPAT margins sufficiently to justify its present valuation, as the reader will see in the discussion on valuation.

The Perverse Effects of a Large Market Opportunity

According to the S-1/A filing, management believes that the HeartFlow Platform is, “the first and most widely-adopted AI-powered test for CAD”, boasting

a competitively differentiated data set of approximately 110 million annotated images, which is primarily sourced from our commercial relationships with customers, driving training and refinement of our algorithms for over 10 years and the ability to train new AI models for future products.

The size of its market is fairly large, with the company estimating that the market opportunity for HeartFlow FFRCT Analysis and HeartFlow Plaque Analysis is currently worth some $5 billion, with “9.5 million unique stable chest pain patients” receiving non-invasive tests ((NITs)) every year. Management estimates that there are 3.1 million people eligible for its HeartFlow FFRCT Analysis, which, at $1,067, leads to a market opportunity of $3.3 billion. The HeartFlow Plaque Analysis is applicable to 60% of those 9.5 million NIT patients a year, and the bulk of patients experiencing acute chest pain. Management expects that HeartFlow Plaque Analysis has a market opportunity of around 5.5 million patients, which, at a sales price of $300, translates to a $1.7 billion incremental market opportunity.

However, large market opportunities attract capital, and this has the effect of driving down returns on invested capital (ROIC) down toward the cost of capital, largely because the profit available for the taking tends to be overestimated, leading to overinvestment, something which HeartFlow itself acknowledges, saying,

Our estimate of the addressable market for our current products and any future products is subject to significant uncertainty and is based on assumptions and estimates, including our internal analysis and industry experience.

As the investor Peter Thiel once said, “competition is for losers”. The enemy of profitability, competition is significant in an era of “rapid technological change”. Management says that,

Because of the size of the market opportunity for the treatment of CAD, we believe current and potential future competitors will dedicate significant resources to aggressively promote their products or develop new products or treatments. Our principal competition comes from companies that provide traditional non-invasive tests that aid physicians in the evaluation of CAD, such as SPECT, stress echocardiography and PET. Established, traditional non-invasive tests for CAD have been used for many years and are therefore difficult to change or supplement. Many of the companies that sell these traditional non-invasive tests or the equipment they require have established relationships with healthcare providers. One of the major hurdles to adoption of our products is overcoming established testing patterns, which requires education of physicians and supportive clinical data. The companies that sell the traditional non-invasive tests for CAD include companies that offer: (i) cardiac specific tests to primary care and cardiology offices, such as manufacturers of capital equipment for stress echocardiography and SPECT, including GE Healthcare, Siemens Healthineers AG and Koninklijke Philips N.V.; and (ii) products used for the invasive FFR testing market. With the greater resources of some of these competitors and their more diversified product offerings, it is possible that they or other entrants into the market may develop competing products or technologies that could be more effective, accurate, reliable, cost-effective, more advanced or otherwise improved relative to the HeartFlow Platform, which could render our products obsolete or less competitive.

The firm also faces competition from firms using AI-driven platforms that use coronary computed tomography angiography (CCTA) to diagnose CAD, such as Cleerly, Inc., Elucid Bioimaging Inc. and Keya Medical Technology Co., Ltd. Furthermore, computed tomography (CT) scanner manufacturers such as Siemens Healthineers AG (OTCPK:SMMNY), GE HealthCare Technologies Inc. (GEHC), Koninklijke Philips N.V. (PHG), Hitachi, Ltd. (OTCPK:HTHIY), Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd., and Canon Medical Systems Corporation, who are larger, more diversified, and better resourced, could develop the capability to compete with or render incompatible the HeartFlow Platform, which needs a CT image from a CT scan in order to function. The import of this competition is that the probability of any material increase in profitability by the firm is very low, even if it can grow at a high rate.

Category I Codes Make HeartFlow Compelling

The often inflexible reimbursement landscape is critical for diagnostic innovators such as HeartFlow. Whereas pharmaceuticals are typically reimbursed according to value, diagnostic tests are typically reimbursed on a cost basis, governed by fixed rates under procedural coding schemes such as the Current Procedural Terminology (CPT) in the United States. This cost-basis structure often fails to account for the value that emerges from innovation and may even penalise high-impact diagnostics. Manufacturers have even complained that diagnostic related groups (DRG) and tariff systems are not updated frequently enough, so that reimbursement falls short of reflecting clinical utility.

Moreover, relations between regulators, payers and developers remain strained, with one systematic review of personalised medicine reimbursement models calling for early, pre-approval dialogue with payers, along with coverage with evidence development or performance-based agreements, in order to smooth the pathway to adoption. Absent such collaboration and adaptive reimbursement frameworks, innovations such as HeartFlow FFRCT Analysis may face difficulties with access or commercial marginalisation, even if they show clinical merit.

To this, management says that,

We believe current reimbursement policies support the adoption of the HeartFlow Platform. Our HeartFlow FFRCT Analysis is reimbursed under a dedicated Category I Current Procedural Terminology (“CPT”) code, effective as of January 1, 2024, and has established coverage policies representing approximately 99% of covered lives in the United States. A Category I CPT code was recently established for HeartFlow Plaque Analysis. It will go into effect on January 1, 2026, and is covered by all seven Medicare administrative contractor (“MACs”). A Category I CPT code designates a procedure or service that uses device(s) with Food and Drug Administration (“FDA”) clearance or approval (when required), is performed by many physicians across the United States for its intended clinical use, aligns with current medical practice, and has documented efficacy in literature. The Category I CPT status for our HeartFlow FFRCT Analysis and HeartFlow Plaque Analysis validates their widespread use and distinguish them from emerging technologies that are assigned Category III CPT codes.

The implication is that HeartFlow’s services are clinically valid and widely accepted, and, thanks to the Category 1 codes, they enjoy payer acceptance, reducing claim denials, and offering smoother deployment. This is a competitive advantage compared to competitors with Category III codes, who are subject to scrutiny and denial, giving HeartFlow a distinct adoption advantage. With the upcoming Category I code for HeartFlow Plaque Analysis, HeartFlow is attractively positioned in payer systems even before its full commercial launch.

Of course, it should be noted that these factors do not guarantee physician adoption or fend off competition, but they do ensure market-access by reducing payer friction. Furthermore, the investor should not confuse reimbursement stability with pricing power, as Medicare sets fixed fees, and private insurers tend to anchor to them.

Pathway Dependence is an Obstacle

Many investors often imagine that quality innovations will be quickly adopted by the market. However, there is persuasive evidence that even where clinical data is persuasive and payment mechanisms are aligned, there is a tendency toward inertia within healthcare systems that thwarts the adoption of superior innovations. Both physicians and institutions are often loath to adopt new innovations, preferring to maintain established clinical routines, even when there is persuasive data supporting novel approaches. This clinical inertia, especially in the management of hypertension, often occurs as healthcare providers typically defer or ignore alerts to adjust treatment, with one study showing that inertia of practice accounts for 66% of resistance to change. Clinical inertia is important not merely at the physician level but at the systemic level. The diffusion of innovation is not simply the product of having superior technology. HeartFlow must overcome both clinical inertia and the absence of social pressures to prompt adoption.

Growth in the Absence of Value

Between 2023 and the last twelve months (LTM), HeartFlow’s revenue compounded by 24.98% a year, although the company’s one-year revenue CAGR was “only” 8.23%, which is below the 14.8% mean and greater than the 5.8% median global base rates.

Growth has not been accompanied by profitability, with the firm’s net operating profit after tax (NOPAT) rising from -$48.18 million in 2023 to -$20.81 million in the LTM. The increase in the firm’s albeit negative NOPAT was the result of its NOPAT margin growing from -55.27% to -15.29% in that time. In tandem with this, a decline in HeartFlow’s invested capital turns, from 3.36 to 2.2, led to its ROIC rising from -185.65% to -33.66%.

Capital allocation is management’s most important task, and its goal is the creation of value. The dollar value of this is known as economic profit, and tells us how much management earned in excess of what could have been earned on the market. HeartFlow has not created sustainable value, generating -$26.61 million in economic losses in the LTM.

The failure to achieve profitability has meant that HeartFlow has not been able to generate positive free cash flow (FCF), with the company’s FCF growing nonetheless from -$100.08 million in 2023 to -$18.37 million in the LTM, with LTM FCF having a yield of -1.2%.

Only Enormous Profitability Can Justify Its Price

Using my reverse discounted cash flow (DCF) model, I unearthed the performance expectations that justify the firm’s valuation at the current price, $30.67 at time of writing, and found that the firm is being priced for a profitability that it has so far failed to deliver.

In order to justify its share price wherein the company has a price-to-economic book value (PEBV) of -9.45, HeartFlow will have to

  • compound revenue at 24.98% annually and,

  • immediately achieve a NOPAT margin of 2.96%.

In so doing, HeartFlow would earn $171.08 billion in revenue, and $5.06 billion in NOPAT by 2056, when the shareholder value per share equals the current share price, and the firm has a market-implied competitive advantage period (MICAP) of 31 years. By way of reference, in the LTM, Siemens Healthineers AG generated €23.38 billion (~$26.99 billion) in revenue and €3.22 billion (~$3.71 billion) in earnings before interest and taxes (EBIT), while GE HealthCare Technologies Inc. generated $19.97 billion in revenue and $3.11 billion in EBIT, and Koninklijke Philips N.V. generated €17.86 billion (~$20.62 billion) in revenue and €1.06 billion ($1.22 billion) in EBIT. For further emphasis, just 2.5% of companies compounded revenue by over 25% for a decade, between 1950 and 2015.

If the firm fails to live up to these expectations, the stock carries significant downsides. For instance, if,

  • revenue compounds by an impressive 20% a year, and,

  • NOPAT margin immediately improves to 5.92%, then,

HeartFlow’s stock is worth $15.94 a share today, a 48.03% downside to the current price, with the firm generating $46.55 billion in revenue and $2.75 billion in NOPAT by 2056. If the firm cannot so rapidly improve its margins or its growth prospects dim, then the potential downsides are even greater.

For instance, if

  • revenue compounds by 16% a year, and,

  • NOPAT margin surges to 11.83%, then,

HeartFlow’s stock is worth $3.26 a share today, a downside of 89.37% to the current price, with the firm generating $11.92 billion in revenue and $705.53 million in NOPAT by 2056.

In these scenarios, HeartFlow grows revenue, NOPAT and FCF, without having to increase its incremental net working capital rate or its incremental total adjusted fixed assets rate. This is highly unlikely though, and any such increases to the rate at which it grows its invested capital would create even greater downsides for investors.

Recommendation

With the significant risks inherent in the business and its current price, I assign a “Sell” rating. While HeartFlow has certainly improved its NOPAT margin in recent years, this has been from a low base, and it is unlikely that it can attain the NOPAT margin levels implied by its valuation, at least within the near-term. This is because pricing pressure, customer bargaining power, and intensifying competition, make it unlikely that the company can achieve and sustain the positive NOPAT margin levels necessary to justify its valuation. Furthermore, the odds of the firm sustaining a high growth rate throughout its MICAP are statistically fairly low. The prospect of even greater competition in an already competitive field, the dependence on a single product, and the vulnerabilities related to customer accounts, narrow the paths to the sustainable growth and profitability implied by the company’s valuation.

Summary of Accounting Adjustments

Here below are details of adjustments made to HeartFlow's S-1/A filing for the LTM:

Income Statement: I made $88.39 million in adjustments to calculate NOPAT, adjustments that are equal to 81.96% of the absolute value of HeartFlow's GAAP net income.

Balance Sheet: I made $158.04 million in adjustments to calculate invested capital with a net increase of $123.82 million. HeartFlow's $102.98 million in excess cash was the largest adjustment, equal to 55.83% of HeartFlow's reported assets.

Valuation: I made $318.58 million in adjustments with a net effect of decreasing shareholder value by $112.52 million, with the fair value of total debt, at $214.4 million, being the largest adjustment to shareholder value, representing nearly 8.34% of HeartFlow’s market cap.

data.title: CME Group: If You Own This Stock, Hold On To It
data.description:
Company Description

CME Group (NASDAQ:CME) operates the world's leading derivatives market, offering a trading platform for futures contracts, options, and other investment options to manage risk. The company earns revenue through Clearing and Transaction Fees, Market Data and Information Services, and Other. The company has a current market cap just shy of $100B and employs a relatively small number of people at close 3,800. Over the past decade, CME Group has a total return of 318%, or 15.4% annually, exceeding the overall market returns, as measured by the S&P 500 (SPY), by a few percentage points each year.

Quality Financial Metrics

Let's take a look and some key quantitative financial metrics for CME Group and assess how the company has performed, as well as where it might go.

CME has grown its revenue per share at a moderate pace over the past ten years, about 6.5% annually. The company saw decent growth during 2015 and 2016, and was flat in 2017 before a hefty increase in 2018 of 17%. Following that jump, there was solid 8% growth in 2019, but the pandemic saw this metric stay flat in 2020 and decline by 4% in 2021. Since that decline, the RPS for CME has grown by an average of 9% per year and is expected to grow by about 6.5% in FY25, driven by an increase in customers using their products to mitigate risk.

CME has a very low return on invested capital, with its highest being in 2017 at just 5.2%. Since that peak, the ROIC was cut in half over the next two years, and dwindled even lower in 2021 and 2022. There's been a small bump in the last two years, but to be honest, I typically want to see this metric in the mid-to-high teens at a bare minimum, so CME falls well short of my usual threshold.

One final metric I want to examine is CME's Net Debt to EBITDA, and compare to some other exchanges, CBOE and ICE. As indicated by the blue bars, CME has done a great job keeping its leverage low, with its Net Debt/EBITDA never surpassing 1 over the last decade. CBOE has done well for itself too, with its highest mark at 1.7 in FY17; while ICE is extremely leveraged with the lowest ratio in FY17 at 2 and its highest in FY23 at 4.5. As you can see, CME is considerably better than ICE in this arena, and matches up closely with CBOE. CME should strive to keep its leverage low in order to maintain a lower risk profile, maintain good financial stability, and keep its cost of borrowing to a minimum.

Dividend Data

CME Group currently pays a quarterly dividend of $1.25 per share, giving it an annual payout of $5.00 per share, and a forward dividend yield of about 1.82%. Also, on top of the quarterly dividend, CME also pays a special dividend at the end of each year, which is why you see fluctuations in the total dividends paid on the chart below. Excluding all variable dividends, CME has some inflation-beating dividend growth, its 10-year dividend growth rate sits at about 9.4%, while its 3 & 5-year dividend growth rates are lower, albeit still respectable, at 8.1% and 8.5%, respectively. The incredibly high payout ratio is obviously concerning, but the company consistently has a high PR, which likely stems from the company's strong and predictable cash flows relating to their clearing and transaction fees along with an increase in their average daily volumes.

Earnings Update

In late July, CME Group announce their Q2 2025 earnings with a double-beat, Non-GAAP EPS was $2.96 per share beating the consensus by $0.04, on revenue of $1.69B which exceeded analyst expectations by $10M, and was an increase of 10.5% YoY. Additionally, GAAP EPS was $0.01 more than estimates, coming in at $2.81 per share.

CME's clearing and transaction fees were up 11% compared to 2024, rising from $1.25B to $1.388B. Within this area, there were increased volumes thanks to a rise in market volatility. In fact, management stated in their most recent 10-Q (page 26), they believe "The higher volatility was a result of continued market uncertainty surrounding the Federal Reserve's interest rate policy decision due to mixed market data". Along those same lines, ongoing geopolitical tensions, coupled with uncertainty of the impact of tariffs also contributed to increased volatility.

Revenue for the market data and information services segment climbed from $175M to $198.1M, a rise of 13%. This increase was primarily driven by a higher customer/subscriber count, combined with price increases which typically take effect every January.

The day of the earnings release the stock earned a small gain of less than 1% and overall since the earnings release, CME's stock price has turned red, by less than 0.50%. The full earnings call can be read/listened to here.

CME's Q3 2025 earnings call is expected around October 22, with GAAP EPS projected to be $2.49 per share, with revenue estimated to be $1.58B. Furthermore, analysts expect normalized EPS to be $2.71 per share. It's worth mentioning, of the 15 analyst that cover CME, 4 of them have revised their EPS estimates higher in the last 90 days, while the remaining 11 of them have reduced their EPS projections. Given the somewhat mixed revisions, it'll be interesting to see where Q3 earnings come in.

Valuation

In order to determine if CME is attractively valued let's look at it through the lens of my Free Cash Flow valuation model. As always, to give a little more context on how to view the chart below, the estimated fair value is calculated by dividing the FCF/share for that year by the trailing 5-year average of the FCF yield. The discount line is 10% below the estimated FV, while the premium is 10% above, and finally, the Trim line is 40% above the approximate FV.

The model suggest the stock is trading for very close to its fair value, with a current stock price of $274, and a fair price of $272. As you can tell by the black line the stock has had a nice run up year-to-date, rising almost 18% thus far, and more than 30% over the past full year. CME has consistently been able to increase its FCF/share as indicated by the increase in the valuation lines. In fact since 2017 its FCF per share has more than doubled from $5.19 to almost $11.50 over the TTM.

Currently, CME earns itself a long-term expected rate of return of a little less than 8%. The pieces of that number are a forward dividend yield of 1.82%, a return to fair value components of -0.20%, and an expected earnings growth rate of 6.23%.

Final Thoughts

CME has performed well over the past decade, outpacing the S&P 500 by a little on average per year. The company has grown their RPS consistently in recent years and strong pricing power should allow that to continue; however, the ROIC is incredibly low which is appears to be typical but is still eye-opening nonetheless. CME has rewarded shareholders with above-average dividend increases and it doesn't stop there with variable payouts at the end of each year as well, as a dividend investor this makes owning CME quite enticing. The recent earnings report showed growth in the main segment, led by increased market volatility, which seems likely to continue but time will tell. Taking all that into account, and with most of it on the positive side, the piece that gives me pause is the long-term expected rate of return of less than 8%. I prefer to own/purchase companies with a rate of return of at least 10%, and thus a HOLD rating for CME seems more appropriate. However, I also believe if you currently own this stock you own a great business, but if you are looking to buy, waiting for a better entry point is the way to go.

data.title: Investors Should Be Pining For 1999
data.description:
There are several similarities between the current market and that towards the end of the Internet boom in 1999. Both markets traded at extreme valuations based on various historical valuation metrics, including price-to-sales ratios, Shiller P/E and market cap to GDP ratios. Both markets had their top 10 largest stocks account for approximately 40% of overall market capitalization, although outside of Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), the names have changed.

And then we have two huge technical paradigms powering most of the gains in equities over the past few years. In the late 1990s it was the Internet boom; now it is the AI revolution. However, that is where the comparisons end. The economic, geopolitical and demographic backdrops were much more supportive of growth in 1999 than they are in 2025. So much so that informed investors should be pining for the overall environment in 1999.

Let's start with demographics, given that they are the 'destiny' to most economists. Back in 1999, the Baby Boomers were in their prime earning years. They were socking away money into 401(k) and spending freely in the economy. Now, more than 10,000 are retiring daily and nearly 6,000 Baby Boomers are passing away every day now. They are no longer contributing to the economy like they were in the late 1990s. They are also a key factor behind increasing Social Security and Medicare payments, two large drivers of the massive fiscal deficit the federal government is running.

It is little wonder that GDP growth in the late 1990s rose at a better than four percent clip annually from 1996-1999. Compared to the anemic growth rates of today. The U.S. economy is projected to deliver around 1.5% GDP growth, after growth of 1.25% in the first half of 2025. This was after averaging approximately 2.7% annual GDP growth from 2022-2024.

And remember, a good portion of this growth was driven by massive fiscal deficit spending which averaged between six to seven percent of GDP over the past several years. This compares to 1999, when the federal government actually ran a small fiscal budget surplus (the last time it has done so). The federal debt closed out the 20th century at under $6 trillion. It has now surpassed $37 trillion and continues to increase at an alarming rate.

One casualty of this slow growth and high debt is a core foundation of the American Dream. As you can see from the graph below, the percentage of 30-year-olds that are married and own their home has dropped off the cliff in recent decades.

Is it any wonder that so many Millennials and Gen Z state they are financially worse off than their parents? One of the reasons for this is the massive increase in overall student loan debt in this century.

The anxiety from these credit obligations is increasing markedly since the administration recently restarted student loan repayments after more than a four-year taxpayer-financed hiatus. This has resulted in a massive increase in student loan delinquency rates. And remember student loans can't be discharged via bankruptcy. They also total more than the record $1.2 trillion in credit card debt Americans are carrying.

Another huge tailwind in the 1990s that is ebbing now is Chinese economic growth. Back at the end of the 20th century, China was just starting to be welcomed into the global trading community. This was a huge growth driver over the next two decades as China grew to be the second-largest economy in the world.

However, that growth is slowing significantly from previous levels as China ages. In addition, China is now the number one economic and military rival to the United States. Much higher tariffs on Chinese goods are a given, although the exact details are still being worked out.

Then we have U.S. tariffs, which are being adjusted to their highest levels in generations. Another huge potential headwind to global economic growth, which is projected to run at an approximate and anemic three percent growth rate in 2025 and 2026.

Investors seem content to put all their eggs in the AI Revolution basket. However, while this is a true paradigm shift since the birth of the internet in the late 1990s. However, as I highlighted in a recent article, the AI revolution has substantial downsides that the Internet boom did not. This includes the massive demands new AI data centers which require billions of dollars to build have on the electrical grid and on freshwater supplies. In addition, the birth of AI is likely going to trigger massive job losses, at least in the short term. A poll this week saw over 70% of Americans believe that AI will lead to permanent large-scale job losses. Not exactly a good omen for consumer confidence in the coming quarters.

So, will all these massive investments in AI pay off? A recent MIT study showed 95% of investment in generative AI has led to zero investment returns to date. Which AI-related firms will be the Webvans or Pets.com of this era's investment boom?

So, as we head into the weekend, I have two questions for investors as markets are sporting valuations near or exceeding those of the end of the Internet boom. Would you rather be investing in 1999 with superior economic tailwinds or with current conditions? And if valuations could not hold up in 1999, leading to the massive Internet bust that ran from early 2000 to the summer of 2002, will things be different this time around?

data.title: Fed Chair Powell Pivots To Cutting Mode
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA and Chris Turner

Front end yields down but back end yields not as comfortable

Chair Powell at Jackson Hole:

"Risks to inflation are tilted to the upside, and risks to employment to the downside—a challenging situation. He noted that when our goals are in tension like this, our framework calls for us to balance both sides of our dual mandate. Our policy rate is now 100 basis points closer to neutral than it was a year ago, and the stability of the unemployment rate and other labour market measures allows us to proceed carefully as we consider changes to our policy stance. Nonetheless, with policy in restrictive territory, the baseline outlook and the shifting balance of risks may warrant adjusting our policy stance."

This was dovish than we in rates strategy thought he'd be. He did not object to a rate cut at all. Obviously, he is keeping his options open, but the key move was the downsizing of the tariff-induced inflation risk, mostly on account of a weakening labour market. The big impact market reaction to “shifting risks may warrant adjusting policy” mostly reflected a growing market fear into the speech that Powell could have been outright hawkish. Front-end yields are lower in yield (sub-3.7%), likely structurally. The 10yr yield too (nearing 4.25%). And the probability for a September cut is back up to 90%, having been at sub-70% prior to his speech.

There is a clear yield curve steepening impulse from this. The front end now has Chair Powell on its side, and yields there should stay down. The market is anticipating an ultimate move towards 3% for the effective fund rate, so the 2yr at 3.7% is still some 70bp above this future floor. The long end is not loving this, though. The 30yr yield is back up to where it was at 3.9%. This likely reflects a suspicion that the Fed could be taking risks with inflation here.

The renewed threat this morning from President Trump to fire Fed governor Lisa Cook (for allegedly falsifying bank documents) is another ingredient to consider, as the Trump quest to morph the Fed dovish continues. The front end won't care about this, as it is slavish to where the fund rate goes. But the back end is a deeper thinker, and can worry about second and third round effects, and especially on medium-term risks potentially being taken on inflation.

The net impact here is a steeper curve, and from both ends is shaping up as the likely prognosis ahead.

Dollar gets hit, and more to come

As soon as FX traders saw the headline reference to ‘adjusting policy’, the dollar came off sharply – falling nearly 1% against some of the major currencies. The speech supports the conclusions made after the July jobs report that the Fed could move in September after all. This speech comes at a time when the market has largely priced out expectations of any further easing from the ECB. And at a time when the market is seriously considering another rate hike from the Bank of Japan in October.

Lower short-dated US rates also feed into the narrative that foreign investors can increase their FX hedge ratios on their holdings of US assets. We’ve got targets of 1.17 and 145 for EUR/USD and USD/JPY at the end of this quarter. And if we’re right about a series of Fed rate cuts into year-end, EUR/USD and USD/JPY could be trading 1.20 and 140, respectively.

EM high-yielding currencies, already favoured by global investors, should continue to do well too. Within the G10 space, we also think the activity currencies should outperform. Bullish steepening of the US yield curve is normally good for commodity currencies. We think AUD/USD could play a little catch-up here. And with equities continuing to do well, seemingly on a Fed cutting rates without a recession, the high beta and undervalued Scandinavian currencies should also do well.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: Markets Rally As Powell Flags Downside Employment Risks At Jackson Hole
data.description:
Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell presented the opening remarks for the annual Jackson Hole Economic Policy Symposium this morning in what was one of his most anticipated public appearances since the July FOMC meeting. His remarks focused on two topics. The first was an assessment of the current macro situation, which now includes the July job revisions, and the second was a description of the changes made to the Fed’s monetary policy framework in a review of the “Statement on Longer-Run Goals and Monetary Policy Strategy” which outlines the central bank's general strategy when conducting monetary policy.

Current Conditions

The first part of Powell’s speech was a brief assessment of current economic conditions and an even briefer note on how this might affect monetary policy decisions over the next few meetings. As mentioned before, this is the most significant update that Powell has given since the July employment data revealed a substantial downward revision to May and June nonfarm payroll gains, which were seen as healthy by the FOMC when they met at the end of July. There had been some dovish remarks from other Fed members in that three-week period, but nothing from Powell himself.

In his remarks, Powell acknowledged that the labor market was being affected by new challenges posed by trade and immigration policy that were affecting both the supply and demand sides of the labor market. Over the past few months, job gains had moderated not just because businesses were seeing an uptick in uncertainty surrounding trade but also because a sharp decline in immigration had limited the increase in the labor supply. Powell described this dynamic in his speech, pointing out that the labor market was in “a curious kind of balance that results from a marked slowing in both the supply of and demand for workers.” Because employment growth was still positive, and the unemployment rate had only seen a gradual increase, there was no rush to make changes to policy.

New data seems to have pushed Powell to become a bit more dovish. When discussing the labor market and, specifically, the July downward revisions, Powell said that the slowdown in job growth “is much larger than assessed just a month ago,” suggesting there was a significant shift in his assessment of labor market demand. This led him to later vocalize that “downside risks to employment are rising,” a soundbite which has become a focus for analysts. In general, it seems that there has been a material shift in the views of the labor market, which was largely expected and applauded by the market.

The remarks also addressed the inflation situation, which has also seen an update through the July CPI report earlier this month. Powell said very plainly that “the effects of tariffs on consumer prices are now clearly visible” as he pointed out that goods inflation had shifted up from the slight declines in 2024. However, his hawkish stance that inflation had already started to move up, and would continue to move up in the next few months, was tempered by his saying that “a reasonable base case if that the effects will be relatively short-lived—a one-time shift in the price level.” If this is really the general view of the Fed, then it stands to reason that FOMC members can be more dismissive of inflation data since they represent transitory tariff price increases. However, Powell ends his remarks on inflation with a more hawkish assertion that “we [the Fed] will not allow a one-time increase in the price level to become an ongoing inflation problem.”

Powell summarizes his comments on current conditions with a short statement on what the implications may be for monetary policy. The brief statement mostly reflects what the FOMC has been saying for the last few meetings: upside inflation risks and downside employment risks are generally balanced, and the Fed is still in data dependence mode. But one stuck out as a slight dovish repositioning:

“…with policy in restrictive territory, the baseline outlook and the shifting balance of risks may warrant adjusting our policy stance.”

The first part of Powell’s comment here signals that the Fed still sees policy as in restrictive territory, and while it is not something that the Fed has left to be unclear, it does help to establish a frame of reference for how the Fed is looking at current rates. The second part is more revealing in that it suggests Powell sees a shift in the balance of risks that may lead to an adjustment away from a more restrictive policy. The past few pauses have been done with a focus on upside inflation risks, so implicitly, the statement suggests downside employment risks are becoming more of a concern, likely because of the July jobs report update.

Monetary Policy Framework Adjustments

The second part of Powell’s speech is focused on the changes made to the Federal Reserve’s monetary policy framework as part of the five-year review of the “Statement on Longer-Run Goals and Monetary Policy Strategy.” This review is significant because it has forced the Fed to consider the COVID-era macro phenomenon, which included runaway inflation and historically tight labor market conditions. Those conditions have caused the Fed to shift from the review done in 2020, which examined how monetary policymaking was done at the effective lower bound (ELB), to the 2025 review, which features higher rates, higher inflation, and (likely) a higher neutral rate. Here are the changes that Powell mentioned in his remarks:

  • The ELB no longer a defining feature: In 2020, the Fed’s framework was shaped around the effective lower bound, reflecting the long post-GFC stretch of near-zero rates and low inflation. That language has now been dropped. While the ELB is still acknowledged as a potential constraint, it is no longer the anchor of the strategy. Instead, the statement underscores that monetary policy is designed to meet its dual mandate across a variety of conditions. This signals more flexibility and reduces the sense that policy will automatically fall back to zero rates and large-scale asset purchases in future downturns.
  • Return to flexible inflation targeting: The Fed also moved away from the 2020 “makeup” strategy, which had committed policymakers to aim for inflation moderately above 2% after periods of undershooting. Powell was blunt in saying that the strategy proved irrelevant once inflation surged in 2021–22. The framework now returns to a plain 2% inflation target, with an emphasis on keeping expectations anchored around that level. The change simplifies communication and reinforces that the Fed will not intentionally tolerate overshooting to offset the past.
  • Removal of “shortfalls” language on employment: The reference to “shortfalls” from maximum employment has been dropped, since it created confusion and, at times, was read as a promise not to act preemptively. The new language clarifies that employment can run above estimates of its sustainable level without creating problems, but that the Fed will not hesitate to respond if labor market tightness fuels inflation risks. This marks a subtle but important shift: the 2020 language was written for a world where the Phillips curve looked flat, but the revision reflects recognition that its slope can change and policy must be able to adapt.
  • Balanced approach reaffirmed: Finally, the statement sharpens how the Fed balances its dual mandate when inflation and employment are not moving in the same direction. The new wording, closer to the 2012 version, highlights that the Fed weighs both the size of the deviations from its goals and the likely time needed to correct them. In practice, this codifies what was seen from 2022 to 2024, when bringing down inflation took precedence, but it leaves room for policy to tilt in the other direction when labor market risks are more pressing.

Taken together, the changes mark a pivot from a framework designed for a low-inflation, low-rate world toward one built for a more uncertain environment, where inflation can be too high as well as too low, labor markets can run hot without necessarily driving prices, and trade-offs between employment and price stability are judged more symmetrically.

Market Reaction

The text of Powell’s speech was released right at 10 am, so financial markets were able to respond immediately to what the Fed Chair would say in the half-hour ahead. As expected, the moves across asset classes were markedly dovish, responding to the changes in the assessment of the labor market:

  • Equity markets started the morning in the green, but enjoyed an extension of the rally in the immediate trading after 10 am. The S&P 500 and NASDAQ are both up around 1.6%, and the Dow Jones is up 2.1%. The Russell 2000 index, which has been especially sensitive to changes in rate expectations in the last few months, surged 3.8% in what could be its largest single-day move since April 9th. The jump today puts it at its highest point since mid-December 2024. Overall, this has been one of the strongest dovish days for the market in 2025.

  • The bond market has also responded dovishly as the yield curve dropped across all maturities. The largest drops in Treasury yields were in the medium-term maturities like the 2-year and the 3-year, each down about -10 bps. The moves in short-term yields were not as large, but both the 3-month and the 6-month rates have dropped to their lowest points in almost three years. Long-term yields like the 10-year and 30-year both fell but remain elevated compared to the short end. As seen in the short-term rate moves, the remarks today at Jackson Hole have made bond traders more dovish in the short-term than they have been in a long time.
  • With a sharp decline in rates, typically comes a similar move in the USD markets. Indeed, the dollar index (DXY) is down -0.9% in the aftermath of the speech, almost completely erasing the gains in the last month. The dollar depreciation is pretty uniform across major currency pairs, as EURUSD, GBPUSD, AUDUSD, and CHFUSD are all up around 0.9% to 1.0%, and USDJPY is down -1.1%.

It appears that a few critical quotes from Powell’s Jackson Hole speech have rejuvenated rate cut expectations and put the decision of a quarter-point cut in September squarely in the lead. According to CME Group’s FedWatch, the cut probability jumped from 75.0% yesterday to 87.2% following Powell’s remarks, slightly higher than where they were a week ago and close to where they were after the July jobs report.

It does seem to me that Powell did signal a shift in the Fed’s view of the balance of risks today, but I don’t think it is really much of a surprise. I expect the Fed to cut in September, but move back to a more careful approach to further easing in the last two meetings of the year. That, of course, assumes there are no major surprises in the data in the rest of Q3 and the Fed’s independence remaining mostly intact until Powell steps down next year.

data.title: Bombardier Stock Surges: I'm Downgrading, But What's Next?
data.description:
Bombardier stock (OTCQX:BDRAF) has rallied past my stock price with a 94% gain since my last report, sharply outperforming the S&P 500’s 12.6% gain. In February, amid tariff concerns, I increased my rating from "Buy" to "Strong Buy" for the Canadian business jet maker, and that has paid off as the stock now has more than doubled. In this report, I review the most recent earnings, highlight the risks and opportunities, and update my price target.

Bombardier Sales And Margins Contract

While Bombardier’s stock soared, second-quarter sales dropped from $2.2 billion to $2 billion, missing analyst estimates by $91.6 million. The decline in sales, however, masks the stellar growth in services revenues, which provides a high margin opportunity to capitalize on the installed base of airplanes. Services sales increased more than 16% to $590 million. Manufacturing revenues declined $258 million to $1.44 billion. This decline was as planned, with deliveries dropping from 39 units to 36 units. The company delivered fewer Global 7500 airplanes, partially offset by more Challenger business jet sales. Given that the sticker price of a Global 7500 business jet is $78 million, delivering fewer can quickly result in lower revenues as well as missing analyst expectations.

Adjusted EBITDA declined 11.3% from $335 million to $297 million, with margins declining from 15.2% to 14.6%. This was driven by the lower Global 7500 deliveries, which come with good margins. Free cash flow usage was $164 million compared to a usage of $68 million a year ago, reflecting a backloaded delivery profile for which the company invests now. While the company missed sales estimates, saw its EBITDA and margins decline and free cash burn widen, the earnings per share of $1.11 beat estimates.

The Positives For Bombardier

While the company didn't include a guidance slide in its earnings presentation, the company did confirm its earlier guidance aiming for more than 150 business jet deliveries this year, revenues of more than $9.25 billion, adjusted EBITDA of more than $1.55 billion and free cash flow between $500 million and $800 million. For Bombardier, there are quite many positives. The company has built an inventory of $850 million that will unwind in the second half of the year and drive cash flow generation. The mix on those deliveries will include more Global business jets. That means besides cash inflow, we'll also see a delivery mix with a better margin, reversing the lower EBITDA margins in the first half of the year.

Second, the company will deliver the first Global 8000 jet in the second half of the year. Those jets come with a $3 million higher sticker price than the Global 7500, and I believe the company will get through the learning curve to mature margins on the Global 8000 faster, driven by the lessons learned on the Global 7500 program.

The company has received an order for two Global 6500 from Saab (OTCPK:SAABF) and announced a memorandum of understanding with Leonardo (OTCPK:FINMY) to explore opportunities for a maritime patrol aircraft based on the Global 6500 platform. These agreements highlight opportunities in defense, while a new commercial customer purchased 50 Challenger and Global jets with 70 options as well as services in a deal valued at $4 billion. Deliveries are set to start in 2027. If market demand remains robust, it could provide the company with an opportunity to significantly increase production.

The company is also increasing its services footprint with expansions in the United States. These expansions can be viewed as a mitigation to trade risk as we see more and more aerospace suppliers setting up a more “local-for-local” network, but it also fits in expansions we've seen over the past few years aiming to capitalize on services opportunities and provide long-term support for the Global 7500/8000 jets that will be delivered in the years ahead.

In the context of trade, we already saw that many aerospace suppliers and OEMs have indicated that impact will be limited through the USMCA trade agreement, commercial negotiations and trade deals tend to exclude aerospace products from tariffs.

With the stellar financial performance at Bombardier, we're also seeing the company refinancing its debt at more attractive interest rates. During the second quarter, the company successfully refinanced $500 million in debt, driving down its interest rate on its total debt by 11 basis points while it also received credit rating upgrades from S&P and Moody’s.

Market For Business Jets: Confidence Slides

Bombardier faces two risks. One of the risks is that aerospace supply chain pressures are not fully behind the company. With a backloaded delivery profile, it could eventually mean that some planned deliveries do slip. However, with an inventory build in the first half of the year, the company has also done its part of risk mitigation.

While the long-term prospects for the market remain healthy, we note that confidence slid in the second quarter below the stability threshold. At the same time, we note that flight hours in all markets are looking solid, showing growth and inventory levels of pre-owned jets are below historic levels. A high inventory level could induce pressure on new-built business jet pricing. However, we also note that an increase in pre-owned inventory could also be reflective of pre-owned jets coming to the market as business jet owners look to acquire new jets and Bombardier has a program to bring pre-owned jets to the market. So, inventories not only provide a potential pressure but also an opportunity for Bombardier to bring Bombardier-certified refurbished jets to the market.

Bombardier Stock Is Now A Buy

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Bombardier stock has reached my prior $97.98 price target. In my most recent assessment, I have the fundamentally supported stock price for 2025 at $99.62. This would imply downside from current trading levels. However, I also think that there are substantial positives, such as the Global 8000 introduction later this year and the substantial order that could improve predictability for the business, allowing to value the stock with future year earnings in mind. We note that 2026 earnings are also reflected in the current share price.

Typically, I value stocks a year ahead of their earnings, which would normally result in a "Hold" rating for Bombardier. However, we note that given the historic weak performance of Bombardier, its median EV/EBITDA is sharply discounted against the peer group. The company, however, has transformed its operations focusing on business jets and is more successful in increasing margins and cash flows, setting the company up for better debt management, which it has already demonstrated.

As a result, as I expect that the EV/EBITDA multiple will further strengthen, I'm valuing the stock another year ahead with a $138.20 price target, noting an expected leverage below 1, which is substantially better than the peer group average of around 1.4x. It could provide the company with a trigger, market conditions permitting, to return capital to shareholders.

Conclusion: Bombardier’s Future Is Looking Bright

While I'm reducing the rating from "Strong Buy" to "Buy" as my investment thesis has largely materialized for the near-term, I'm maintaining a "Buy" rating with a forward-looking price target of $138.20. I believe that with a better view on tariff impacts, a substantial order for which deliveries start in 2027, there is the potential for Bombardier to return value to shareholders while its financial performance could trigger the market to value the company closer to peers, which would unlock substantial upside.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Walmart: Currently More Expensive Than Amazon
data.description:
Introduction

Walmart Inc. (NYSE: NYSE:WMT) has been a staple of American groceries for decades, as it built the vastest network of hypermarkets and grocery stores across the U.S. while having its humble beginnings in rural Arkansas (I highly recommend Sam Walton’s book). It is now the largest company in the world in terms of revenue, shaping the retail landscape itself.

The company has just released its Q2 earnings, and I believe that now is the right time to offer a fresh review on one of the greatest companies that ever existed, in my mind.

Operation overview

Walmart U.S. remains the bread and butter of the company with its current omnichannel strategy, combining over 4,600 stores and a strong digital presence. The number of stores is close to 2.5 times that of rival Target (NYSE: TGT), with stores located within 10 miles of 90% of the U.S. population. The company is heavily entrenched in the U.S. retail industry, as many companies rely on Walmart’s access to customers to drive sales. Furthermore, Walmart’s share of superstore visits in the U.S. was nearly 60% in 2022, far outpacing rivals. Walmart is aggressively investing in automation for its supply chain and e-commerce fulfillment operations, as to increase volumes and reduce both delivery time and costs. The segment has generated $465.9B in sales over the LTM, up from $369.96B in 2021.

Walmart International has around 5,400 stores across 18 countries which has been a good diversification effort with a focus on key markets such as Mexico and e-commerce expansion. Other markets include Canada and China which are standout performers, and Walmart plans on adapting store formats and product assortments while also mirroring the U.S. strategy operational focus. With the current global trade dynamics, Walmart International is diversifying its supplier base, with a shift in terms of sourcing from countries such as India and Vietnam to mitigate risks. The segment now accounts for $121.8B for the LTM, up from $100.98B in 2023.

Sam’s Club is the membership-only warehouse division that has been undergoing a strategic shift aiming at enhancing value and experience through expanded footprint and technology to catch up with Costco (NASDAQ: COST). Recently, the segment has announced that it will open 30 new locations and a total remodel of all 600 of its existing clubs. Sam’s club has generated $90.87B in sales for the LTM.

Q2 earnings and Current Dynamics

Walmart is currently down over 4% on its earnings release as the company has published a rare earnings miss at a Q2 Non-GAAP EPS of 68 cents, a 6-cent miss. Though revenue came in at $177.4B, a beat of over 3B, and a 4.8% Y/Y. Walmart also raised its outlook for net sales growth to between 3.75% and 4.75% and adjusted EPS up to $2.62 for the company’s fiscal 2026.

There are some strong takeaways based on the company’s goal to ramp up e-commerce, with sales growing by 25% thanks to pickup & delivery and marketplace. The miss was the first time since May 2022, as the company felt pressure on profits, such as one-time expenses like restructuring costs and litigation settlements. Walmart also saw an elevated self-insured liability cost. The significant drop in share price has been primarily due to the elevated P/E ratio, which leaves little room for maneuver and adds volatility to any negative news.

Though after 5 decades of growth, Walmart seems to be near the peak of its possible physical presence, with the company pushing its omnichannel strategy to customers. The main dynamic has been how Walmart can compete with Amazon (NASDAQ: AMZN), as its third-party fulfilment capacity is pale relative to Amazon’s scale. Amazon can still underprice Walmart relative to commissioning and listing fees on its marketplace. Sam’s Club has also underperformed Costco in recent years, and the brand does not command the same kind of loyalty that Costco does.

Walmart+ has expanded drastically since 2020, as it was aimed at boosting brand and digital engagement. The program costs $98 a year and offers free home delivery, fuel benefits, and Paramount+ streaming. This is a clear attempt by the retailer to build its ecosystem like Amazon, though it has the added advantage of its large physical footprint.

Valuation

Walmart is certainly not cheap, as highlighted by its P/E Non-GAAP of 38.72, over 125% higher than the sector median. Furthermore, the company has an expensive EV/EBIDA of 19.92, close to over 65% higher than the sector median. Walmart is trading vastly higher than its historical average, with its historical P/E ranging between 10 and 15 pre-2018. I believe that it’s on the back of the current flight to safety in this uncertain economy, with many investors prioritizing investments in a company such as Walmart that seems to be recession-proof.

Walmart is simply the whale in its industry, with its sheer scale and its position as the go-to for value, which gives it a powerful moat. The company currently has a FCF yield of 1.76%. It currently astonishes me that Amazon is cheaper than Walmart despite having some of the strongest prospects outside of its retailing business, in AWS and its AI ventures.

Growth has been strong as seen in its recent earnings, with e-commerce sales up 25% in the quarter, and has accelerated from its historical averages. I believe that the market is a bit too enthusiastic about Walmart’s ability to maintain its current growth trajectory amid its current market saturation and immense competition, especially considering its overvaluation. However, Sam’s Club has shown some evidence that it was able to turn around its past performance with good member growth over the past couple of years.

For profitability, Walmart has some of the best metrics in the sector thanks to Sam Walton’s original motto of always cutting down costs. More so, its vast scale and omnichannel distribution allow the firm to adapt in real time to retail dynamics and maintain its robust profitability. This has allowed Walmart to keep its gross margins around 25% for the past 10 years, despite adding complexities to the business. Walmart also has a stellar ROIC at 11.01% while drastically improving its ROE over the past 10 years to now reaching close to 23% over the past twelve months.

Regarding capital return to shareholders, Walmart currently has a poor dividend yield of 0.93%, which has been growing at a rate of 3.45% CAGR over the past 10 years. Walmart is a dividend aristocrat with over 50 consecutive years of annual dividend increases as well. In the past quarter, the company has repurchased around $1.6B of its shares, with over $5.9B remaining under its current repurchase authorization.

Conclusion

Walmart is a great company that has one of the largest scales and presence in the U.S. Its omnichannel strategy is a good fit for the company and is/should drive sales growth in the future while keeping the company's margins intact. Saying that, I believe that the company is simply overpriced at its current state, posting higher valuation metrics than Amazon or even Alphabet. I rate the company as a Hold.

data.title: Black Rock Coffee Bar Begins IPO Rollout
data.description:
Black Rock Is Growing And Producing Operating Profit

Black Rock Coffee Bar, Inc. (BRCB) has filed to raise $100 million in an IPO of its Class A common stock, according to SEC S-1 registration information.

BRCB operates a growing network of coffee and related products retail stores in the United States.

Store operating margin is increasing even as the company continues to expand its footprint in the western US.

I’ll provide a final opinion when we learn more IPO information from management.

What Does Black Rock Do?

Scottsdale, Arizona-based Black Rock Coffee Bar, Inc. was founded to develop improved coffee and food experiences in various retail formats in the US.

Management is led by Chief Executive Officer Mark Davis, who has been with the firm since 2023 and was previously Chief Operating Officer of Coffee & Bagel Brands and, before that, CEO of Tokyo Joe's.

The company's primary offerings include:

  • Coffee.

  • Energy drinks.

  • Food products.

The firm operated 158 stores in seven states as of June 30, 2025.

BRCB’s revenue contribution by store type and number of stores is shown in the pie charts here:

Black Rock has booked a fair market value investment of $254.3 million as of June 30, 2025, from investors, including Viking Cake BR, The Cynosure Group and Daniel Brand.

What Is Black Rock’s Market?

According to a 2025 market research report by Future Market Insights, the global market for out-of-home coffee is an estimated $24.5 billion in 2025 and is expected to exceed $41.8 billion by 2035.

This represents a projected CAGR of 5.5% from 2026 to 2035.

The primary factors driving this expected growth are evolving consumer preferences for premium and functional drinks, distribution improvements and rebounding out-of-home experience demand.

Also, the US out-of-home coffee market is expected to grow at a CAGR of 4.6% from 2025 to 2035, 'driven by strong cafe culture and widespread adoption of grab-and-go formats…'Premiumization continues to gain traction, with cold brews and nitro coffees becoming mainstream.'

Major competitive vendors or industry players include the following firms:

  • Starbucks Corporation (SBUX).

  • Dutch Bros Inc. (BROS).

  • Dunkin'.

  • Scooter's Coffee.

  • Peet's Coffee.

  • Caribou Coffee.

  • 7 Brew Coffee.

  • Biggby Coffee.

  • Others.

The firm is currently growing its store and drive-thru footprint of all corporate-owned stores in major western US states.

Management expects to execute average annual unit growth of 20% annually to achieve its goal of 1,000 stores by 2035.

I’ve prepared a thumbnail SWOT analysis for Black Rock:

Strengths

  • Strong unit economics even as the company expands quickly, differentiated products, and a growing loyalty program.

Weaknesses

  • Geographic concentration, dependence on drive-thru format, and reliance on branded products.

Opportunities

  • Store footprint growth, product category expansion, and demand for premium energy drinks.

Threats

  • Intense competition from established coffee chains, an uncertain macroeconomic environment that affects discretionary spending, and operational risks from supply chain or labor shortages.

Black Rock’s Recent Financial Results

Black Rock’s IPO Plan

BRCB intends to sell shares of Class A common stock in an IPO that will likely exceed $100 million.

No existing or possibly new shareholders have indicated an interest in acquiring additional shares, at least as disclosed in the initial prospectus.

Class A stockholders will receive one vote per share.

Class B shareholders will have one vote per share and Class C shareholders will be entitled to ten (10) votes per share.

Class B and C shareholders will not have any economic rights (e.g., dividends) and will not receive any distribution in the event of a winding up of the company.

Company, shareholder and executive lock-ups will be for the normal and ordinary 180-day period after the effective date of the prospectus.

According to Black Rock’s most recent regulatory filing, it plans to use the net proceeds as follows:

Black Rock OpCo currently intends to use the net proceeds it receives from this offering, together with proceeds from the Refinancing and the Co-Founder Contribution, [i] to redeem certain of its currently outstanding redeemable preferred LLC units [...], [ii] to repay all ... outstanding borrowings under the Credit Facility and, [iii] to the extent there are remaining proceeds, for general corporate purposes.

(Source - SEC)

The firm’s presentation for the company roadshow is not yet available.

Pertaining to potential legal exposure, leadership said any current legal claims would not have a material adverse effect on its operations or financial position.

Listed underwriters of the IPO are J.P. Morgan, Jefferies, Morgan Stanley, Baird, Stifel, Raymond James and William Blair.

Black Rock Is Growing Revenue and Operating Profit

Black Rock is seeking US public capital market investment for its continued store growth plans.

The firm’s financials have generated faster topline revenue growth, growing store operating profit, increasing store operating margin and higher operating profit and cash flow from operations.

Free cash flow for the twelve months ended June 30, 2025, was negative ($9.4 million).

Selling, G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue have remained stable despite growing its footprint and its Selling, G&A efficiency multiple rose to 1.3x in the most recent reporting period, a positive result.

The firm currently plans to pay no dividends and will keep any future earnings for its expansion plans and working capital needs.

BRCB’s recent capital spending history shows it has spent heavily on capital expenditures as it continues to open new stores at a fast pace.

The firm’s growth strategy features geographic expansion targeting 30 store openings in 2025, menu innovation for seasonal promotions and exclusive beverages and further brand development activities.

The market opportunity for out-of-home coffee and related products is large and expected to grow at a moderate rate of growth in the coming years as consumers continue to seek improved experiences.

Risks to the company’s outlook as a public firm include its concentration in the western US, its focus on drive-thru areas, and 24% of its revenue tied to a single beverage category (Fuel).

When we get more information from management about the IPO’s pricing and valuation assumptions, I’ll provide an update.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced

data.title: Zoom Post-Earnings: Sell The 10% Gain 'Cause We See Cracks (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Zoom Communications Inc. (NASDAQ:ZM) reported 2QFY26, causing the stock to trade up about 5% in pre-market trading and increased to up 10% at market open, bumped up by Fed Chair Jerome Powell hinting at rate cuts at Jackson Hole. The report gave investors a much-needed breath of fresh air, with the stock struggling to maintain an upward trajectory; since the April lows of around $66 per share, the stock hit a high of $84 before shedding 13% and revisiting $73 at Thursday’s close. The stock is down 10% since the start of the year, and it’s been on the same downward trend since COVID highs in 2020, having consolidated in the same range for the past three to four years. This isn’t a stock for long-term holding, in our opinion, at least not at current levels, considering the big elephant in the room: a messy macro backdrop, higher jobless claims, and lackluster online churn in spite of new AI features.

Earnings recap:

The company reported adjusted EPS of $1.53, higher than $1.39 reported in a year ago quarter on revenue of $1.22B, up 4.7% Y/Y. Zoom beat on top and bottom lines and ended the quarter with 4,274 customers contributing more than $100K in trailing 12-month revenue. This figure is up 8.7% Y/Y with a 12-month dollar expansion rate for its enterprise customers sitting around 98%, underpinning the stickiness of the platform. Online average monthly churn came in at 2.9%, which is flat from a year ago quarter but higher than the past three quarters, which isn't a good sign. The overhang for Zoom isn’t the platform itself; it's the broader macro backdrop. Higher jobless claims that came in on Thursday and a continued layoff environment this year put together a less-than-rosy picture for the likes of Zoom.

Total revenue has picked up sequentially and from a year ago quarter, supported by better enterprise revenue up 3.7% Q/Q and 7% Y/Y. Management is pushing the AI narrative as the game-changer on this front, with new releases early last month, but we think the problem is more related to the macro picture than to the stickiness of the platform itself. Confirming our concern is the company’s miss on current remaining performance obligation or RPO, which came in at $2.41B, lower than the $2.45B expected. Similarly, remaining performance obligations or RPO also came in below expectations at $3.98B, lower than the expected $4B. These are barely misses, but we do think the market could easily get ahead of itself after the Q2 print, setting up Zoom for higher risk next quarter.

Data from the Labor Department earlier this week points to cracks in the U.S. job market. New unemployment claims jumped last week by 11,000 to a seasonally adjusted 235,000, marking the sharpest rise since late May. Economists had been looking for a smaller uptick, around 225,000. The increase comes as hiring momentum has cooled and layoffs appear to be edging higher. This spells a less-than-friendly environment for services like Zoom. Not to mention pushes from companies like Intel (INTC) to go back in the office starting in September, which decreases the demand for communication services such as Zoom.

Over the past three months, job growth has averaged just 35,000 per month, reflecting businesses’ caution as they contend with Trump’s tariff-heavy trade stance, which has pushed import duties to levels not seen in a century. We believe we still exist in a corporate America that’s strapped for cash and, hence, see less momentum for players like Zoom. We expect this to change into 2026, but believe that the near-term risk-reward for Zoom is less than favorable, particularly as this quarter's results and AI commentary boosted market expectations.

AI is moving the needle, but not enough

Back on July 9th, Zoom launched new AI-based capabilities for its platform to help optimize time and task efficiency, including the Custom AI Companion add-on, building on other features using AI such as meeting summaries, real-time meeting questions, and composing emails or chats via AI. This quarter, we’re seeing AI enhancing customer value with Zoom CEO Eric S. Yuan noting on the call:

AI Companion monthly active users have grown over 4x year-over-year, with millions using our AI to boost business value throughout the meeting lifecycle and beyond…AI adoption is increasing within our customer experience offering and transforming how brands engage their customers and build loyalty with our set of modern, differentiated, AI-first tools. You see this momentum in the number of Zoom Contact Center customers with over $100,000 ARR, which grew 94% year-over-year to 229, highlighting our ability to win with large accounts in high-stakes deployments and migrate them into the high-end AI products.

The results of AI-driven adoption of features are a big win, and the traffic among larger players shouldn’t be overlooked. Our concern is that AI wins need time to play out and need a healthier backdrop to justify customer spend, particularly for new joiners. There is a risk that investors' excitement about Zoom’s AI-driven adoption is also heightened and potentially too rushed, particularly in light of the recent MIT study that found about 95% of AI initiatives at companies fail to turn into profit.

Valuation & What to do?

It feels that Zoom is in a good place for the first time in a minute, but we don’t see enough moat for the good news to continue, particularly looking at underlying metrics like online churn and RPO. Management is guiding ahead of expectations for Q3, forecasting revenue between $4.83B to $4.84B ahead of consensus for $4.81B. The repurchase of $6M in shares of common stock during Q2 is a positive signal, but not one that pushes the needle against a cautious macro backdrop, yet. Valuation points to this mixed outlook. The stock is trading at a forward EV/Sales of 2.99x, against the sector average of 3.05x, and a Price/Sales ratio of 4.60x, versus the average of 3x. The stock has underperformed the S&P 500 since our last hold rating in February by around 8%. The stock also underperforms the S&P 500 since our upgrade to hold back in October 2023, up 17% versus the S&P 500, up 47% during the same period. We last wrote on Zoom, we said two things had become clear about the company: it’s unlikely to recapture its glory days during the pandemic, and AI monetization will lag without a healthier macro backdrop. We think these two things remain true post-Q2 earnings.

The stock doesn’t appear set up for bullish momentum in the short-term from a technical point of view, with EMAs bearishly stacked. Things are likely to change at market open if EMA21 crosses EMA200.

Having said that, we don’t see room for a meaningful reversal in the broader downward trend the stock is caught in. We wouldn’t recommend investors bet on it amid the current backdrop and see an attractive window to trim on post-earnings gains.

data.title: BITU - How Trump's Crypto Pivot Supports This Leveraged Play
data.description:
Bitcoin bull cycles follow halving events and last 12-18 months. The last halving event was in April 2024 so, by all calculations, we are at the end of the rally. Utilizing a 2x leveraged bitcoin ETF like the ProShares Ultra Bitcoin ETF (NYSEARCA:BITU) is not a good idea at this time because it magnifies the correction risk.

However, certain factors make me reconsider that idea. The key factor is that unlike past halving events, this time we have a President who is such a crypto enthusiast that he has a new meme coin to his name - the $TRUMP. President Trump - unlike his predecessors - has eased bitcoin regulation in the US and billions of dollars have started flowing into the sector. Moreover, The European Union has enacted a framework of bitcoin regulation - MiCA or Markets in Crypto-Assets Regulation - which helps draw in large investors. Finally, federal interest rate easing is also a tailwind.

Of course, I think we do need to reconcile the historical “end of run” narrative with this extension thesis more conservatively. Even if supportive policies extend inflows by only $10–15 billion annually (a small fraction of the proposed 401k allocation), that may translate to a more modest extra upside, not the multi-fold explosions of earlier cycles. This keeps expectations grounded while allowing for upside beyond the usual 12–18 months.

Taking the bullish view together with the caveats, I believe these factors may be able to sustain this bull run beyond the usual 12-18 months. If that is so, using a 2x leveraged ETF like BITU may pay off, doubling your returns. Here’s a discussion of the thesis, and some suggestions on how best to play BITU as an active and alert trader.

Bitcoin - ordinary runs vs this run

We have had four bitcoin bull runs driven by halving events. Price appreciation data below:
